topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The accusation landed on Liam’s desk like a damp cloth. He picked up the printed statement, the crisp paper feeling strangely weightless in his hand. He didn't flinch. His gaze drifted to the window, where the afternoon sun was doing nothing remarkable. "Plagiarism," the dean had stated, his voice carrying the practiced cadence of someone who believed in the severity of the charge. Liam merely nodded, a small, almost imperceptible movement. He turned back to his laptop, the screen illuminating his face with a flat, unreadable expression. He started typing, the rhythm of his fingers steady, completely detached from the turmoil swirling around him. It was just another administrative hurdle, a temporary inconvenience to the larger, unconcerned landscape of his own priorities.

[story 2]

"Are you denying the source, Mr. Hayes?" the professor asked, leaning across the mahogany desk. Liam looked up slowly, his eyes meeting the professor's with a steady, neutral quality. He didn't offer an apology, nor did he launch into a defense. Instead, he simply smoothed a nonexistent wrinkle on his sweater sleeve. A faint sigh escaped him, barely audible, a sound more like air escaping a punctured tire than genuine distress. He felt a strange detachment, as if watching a distant play. He considered the accusation for a moment, cataloging it as just another piece of data. Then, he picked up his pen, the movement deliberate and slow, and began to sketch out a completely unrelated diagram in the margin of his notes.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the campus library hummed, a monotonous drone that seemed to perfectly match the tenor of Liam’s mood. He sat hunched over his textbook, the weight of the evidence—or accusation—feeling utterly trivial. When the department head finally called him in, Liam rose from his chair without haste. He kept his hands loosely clasped in front of him, his posture relaxed to the point of seeming carelessly slumped. "I understand the gravity of the situation," he replied, his tone even, devoid of any alarm. He looked around the sterile office, taking in the beige walls and the potted, dusty ficus. It was all just scenery. He waited for the inevitable proceedings to conclude, his mind already drifting toward the upcoming weekend plans, completely unburdened by the immediate pressure.
